title: The Blockchain Pitch
flavor: 8:47 AM. The CEO slides into your DMs.
description: CEO: 'What if we just... added blockchain to the onboarding flow? It would really differentiate us.' The sprint ends in 4 days.

choices.consequence: You just added 6 weeks of scope. The devs are crying quietly at their desks.
The CEO nods slowly. Books a 'quick sync' for Thursday. There is no quick sync.

choices.text: Create a JIRA ticket for it
choices.consequence: The ticket sits in the backlog forever. Strategic victory.

title: The Standup Spiral
flavor: 9:03 AM. Jenkins is explaining his weekend. Again.
description: The daily standup is 47 minutes in. Jenkins is live-debugging in the call. Three engineers have muted themselves. Someone is definitely doing laundry.

choices.consequence: You bond over shared suffering. Productivity: aspirational.
Three engineers unsubscribe from your Slack DMs. Worth it.
You now have 47 action items. Most are Jenkins' weekend plans.

title: The One Small Banner
description: Marketing: 'We just need one small banner on the homepage. Maybe with an animation. And a countdown timer. Oh, and can it be personalized?'

choices.consequence: The 'small' banner consumes the entire sprint. The timer is broken on mobile.
Next sprint also has no room. The banner lives in the backlog now.
Marketing goes quiet for 3 days trying to answer. You win... for now.

title: Production is Down
description: Alerts firing. Users tweeting. The CEO has seen the tweet. You have exactly zero context on what's broken.

choices.text: Drop everything, join the war room
choices.consequence: Crisis contained in 2 hours. Status page updated. You haven't eaten.

choices.text: Delegate to eng lead, write updates
choices.consequence: The issue is fixed. Your status updates were impeccable. Team respects you.
The tweet gets 200 angry replies. The CEO DMed you.

title: The Refactor Request
flavor: 2:55 PM. The lead dev has a PowerPoint.
description: Lead dev: 'We need 2 weeks to refactor the auth system. It's held together with duct tape. If we don't do this now, we will regret it in Q3.' They are right. Q3 is soon.

choices.consequence: Future you thanks present you. Current stakeholders do not.

choices.text: Negotiate: half a sprint for refactor
choices.consequence: Everyone is mildly unhappy. That means you found the right answer.
Sprint velocity is great! You now carry a ticking time bomb.

title: The Pivot Moment
description: 80% of users skip the core feature you've been building for 3 months. The research is clear. Your roadmap is not.

choices.consequence: You course-correct. The team questions every decision made since founding.
More research ordered. The quarter ticks by. Data is coming.
Feature ships. Users continue to ignore it. The research was right.

title: The Estimate War
description: Designer says the new component takes 3 days. Developer says 2 weeks. Both are pinging you simultaneously. The sprint plan is a work of fiction.

choices.consequence: You discover they're both right for completely different reasons. Progress.
The designer is offended. The feature ships in 2 weeks. As predicted.

choices.text: Tell them to figure it out
choices.consequence: They do not figure it out. They each file separate Slack complaints.

title: They Shipped It First
description: A competitor just launched your exact feature. Same name. Same positioning. Two days before your planned launch.

choices.consequence: You find a genuine angle. Turns out yours is actually better. Barely.
Team respects your transparency. Also now mildly demoralized.
You saw it. Everyone saw it. It's in the #general channel. You saw it.

title: Zero Users
description: You launched yesterday. The analytics dashboard shows 0 active users. 0 events. 0 page views. Either no one came, or the tracking is broken. You genuinely cannot tell which.

choices.consequence: Tracking was broken. You had 400 users. They were just invisible.
Correct. But you still don't know your real numbers.
Investors see the tweet. That was a Thursday. It is now a difficult Friday.

title: The 9 PM Ping
description: A senior stakeholder messages you after hours: 'Quick question — when do we think the dashboard feature lands? Just want to set expectations.' It is never a quick question.

choices.consequence: They reply with 4 more questions. It is now 10:30 PM.

choices.text: Set Do Not Disturb, ignore it
choices.consequence: Your boundary is respected. Tomorrow will be interesting.
Professional. Aspirational. You set the sync for 9 AM and it gets cancelled.

title: Sprint Planning Tomorrow
flavor: 8:45 PM. The planning doc is blank.
description: Sprint planning is tomorrow at 9 AM. You have: 0 user stories written, a vague memory of last quarter's OKRs, and a team expecting direction.

choices.consequence: The planning is crisp. Efficient. The team is impressed. You are exhausted.

choices.text: Wing it with last sprint's template
choices.consequence: Engineers notice. They say nothing. They write it in the retro doc.
They do. But they planned 3 months of work into a 2-week sprint.

title: The Retro Gets Real
flavor: 5:15 PM. Someone typed something in the retro board.
description: 'We keep saying we'll improve communication but nothing changes.' The room goes quiet. Then everyone starts talking at once. The Miro board has 47 sticky notes.

choices.consequence: Two real commitments emerge. The team feels heard. Small miracle.
Everyone feels better. Nothing is resolved. But feelings were felt.

choices.text: Add it to the parking lot
choices.consequence: The parking lot has 47 items. Nothing has ever left the parking lot.

title: OKR Deadline Day
description: Company OKRs were due today. You have contributed approximately nothing. The shared spreadsheet has your name in a row. It is red.

choices.consequence: Done! They're ambitious, vague, and unmeasurable. Perfect OKRs.
Nobody notices. One of last quarter's OKRs was 'launch the thing'. It shipped.

choices.text: Be honest: ask for 2 more days
choices.consequence: Extension granted. Your OKRs will be thoughtful. And 2 days late.

title: The Big Demo — Day 5
description: Quarterly all-hands demo starts in 15 minutes. Jenkins just pushed to main. Production is giving mixed signals. Investors are in the virtual waiting room.

choices.consequence: Demo goes flawlessly. Investors clap. Jenkins is confused but okay.
Safe. But you'll be explaining this to the board for a month.
Two bugs surface live. You call them 'features in review'. Some believe you.

title: The 'Simple' Feature
flavor: 1:30 PM. The engineering lead requests a word.
description: The feature you told the board would ship 'by end of quarter' just got re-estimated at 3 weeks. The quarter ends in 6 days. Your lead dev looks sympathetic.

choices.consequence: You buy 2 weeks. You have resolved nothing. The clock resets.
Stakeholders are upset. Team respects you deeply. This is the right call.

choices.text: Ship a partial MVP, call it v1
choices.consequence: v1 lands. It's 40% of the feature. Users don't know what they're missing.
